people.bio: Bribed by Philistines to discover Samson's strength. Had his head shaved; the LORD departed.

people.name: Abimelech (son of Gideon)
people.role: Self-appointed king at Shechem
people.bio: Murdered 70 brothers. The anti-judge. Killed by a millstone (Judg 9).

people.name: Joab
people.role: Commander of David's army
people.bio: David's nephew and ruthless general. Killed Abner, Absalom, and Amasa. Executed by Solomon.

people.name: Nathan
people.role: Court prophet to David
people.bio: Delivered the Davidic covenant and confronted David: "You are the man!" (2 Sam 12:7).

people.name: Uriah the Hittite
people.role: Mighty man; Bathsheba's husband
people.bio: Loyal soldier murdered by David to cover adultery. The pivot point of 2 Samuel.

people.name: Abner
people.role: Commander of Saul's army
people.bio: Backed Ish-Bosheth then defected to David. Murdered by Joab (2 Sam 3).

people.name: Hilkiah (father of Jeremiah)
people.role: Priest at Anathoth; father of Jeremiah
people.bio: Hilkiah was a priest from Anathoth in the territory of Benjamin, a Levitical city assigned to the descendants of Aaron. He was the father of the prophet Jeremiah. Anathoth had been the home of Abiathar, the priest exiled by Solomon (1 Kgs 2:26), suggesting Jeremiah's family may have descended from this priestly line. Not to be confused with Hilkiah the high priest who discovered the Book of the Law during Josiah's temple renovation (2 Kgs 22:8).
people.scriptureRole: Mentioned in the superscription of Jeremiah (Jer 1:1) as the father of the prophet, establishing Jeremiah's priestly lineage.

people.name: Baruch son of Neriah
people.role: Scribe and companion of Jeremiah
people.bio: Baruch son of Neriah was Jeremiah's faithful scribe and secretary, responsible for recording the prophet's oracles on a scroll (Jer 36:4). When King Jehoiakim burned the first scroll, Baruch rewrote the entire collection plus additional material (Jer 36:32). He accompanied Jeremiah during the fall of Jerusalem and into forced exile in Egypt. His own lament ('Woe to me! The LORD has added sorrow to my pain,' Jer 45:3) reveals a man who shared the prophet's suffering. A bulla (seal impression) bearing the inscription 'Belonging to Berekhyahu son of Neriyahu the scribe' — almost certainly Baruch's personal seal — was discovered in 1975, providing rare archaeological confirmation of a biblical figure.
people.scriptureRole: Baruch represents the faithful scribe who preserves God's word against political opposition. His role in rewriting the burned scroll (Jer 36) illustrates the indestructibility of divine revelation.

people.name: Jehoiakim
people.role: King of Judah (609–598 BC); son of Josiah
people.bio: Jehoiakim (original name Eliakim) was placed on the throne by Pharaoh Neco after Josiah's death at Megiddo (2 Kgs 23:34). Unlike his reforming father, Jehoiakim was characterized by extravagance, injustice, and contempt for prophetic authority. He built a lavish palace using forced labor (Jer 22:13-17) and famously burned Jeremiah's scroll column by column as it was read to him (Jer 36:23) — a dramatic act of defiance against God's word. He initially served Egypt, then submitted to Babylon, then rebelled — a series of miscalculations that led directly to the Babylonian siege. He died in 598 BC under mysterious circumstances, possibly assassinated.
people.scriptureRole: Jehoiakim is the archetypal king who rejects God's word. His burning of Jeremiah's scroll (Jer 36) is the paradigmatic act of suppressing divine revelation. Jeremiah's prophecy that he would have 'the burial of a donkey' (Jer 22:19) marks him as one of Judah's most condemned rulers.

people.name: Zedekiah
people.role: Last king of Judah (597–586 BC); son of Josiah
people.bio: Zedekiah (original name Mattaniah) was installed as a puppet king by Nebuchadnezzar after the deportation of Jehoiachin in 597 BC (2 Kgs 24:17). He was weak and vacillating — secretly sympathetic to Jeremiah but unable to resist the war party among his officials. He consulted Jeremiah privately (Jer 37:17, 38:14-28) but could not bring himself to follow the prophet's counsel to surrender to Babylon. His final rebellion triggered the devastating siege of 588-586 BC. Captured while fleeing, he was forced to watch his sons executed before his eyes were put out — the last thing he ever saw. He was taken in chains to Babylon, where he presumably died.
people.scriptureRole: Zedekiah embodies the tragedy of weak leadership — knowing the truth but lacking the courage to act on it. His tragic end (Jer 39:6-7, 52:10-11) fulfills Jeremiah's and Ezekiel's prophecies simultaneously: he went to Babylon (Jer 32:5) but never saw it (Ezek 12:13).

people.name: Nebuchadnezzar II
people.role: King of Babylon (605–562 BC); destroyer of Jerusalem
people.bio: Nebuchadnezzar II was the most powerful ruler of the Neo-Babylonian Empire and the dominant figure in the geopolitics of Jeremiah's era. He defeated Egypt at the Battle of Carchemish in 605 BC, establishing Babylonian supremacy over the Levant. He conducted three deportations from Judah (605, 597, 586 BC) and destroyed Jerusalem and the temple in 586 BC. Remarkably, Jeremiah calls him 'my servant' (Jer 25:9, 27:6, 43:10) — God's instrument of judgment against Judah. His construction projects, including the Ishtar Gate and the legendary Hanging Gardens, made Babylon the greatest city of the ancient world. The book of Daniel portrays his encounter with Israel's God and his temporary madness (Dan 4).
people.scriptureRole: Nebuchadnezzar is the human agent of divine judgment in Jeremiah — called God's 'servant' three times. His destruction of Jerusalem fulfills the prophetic warnings of chapters 1-25. In Daniel, he becomes a figure of conversion: the pagan emperor who acknowledges the God of Israel.

people.name: Pashhur son of Immer
people.role: Chief officer of the temple; persecutor of Jeremiah
people.bio: Pashhur son of Immer was the chief officer (pāqîd nāgîd) of the temple in Jerusalem, responsible for maintaining order in the sacred precincts. When Jeremiah returned from smashing a clay flask at Topheth and proclaimed judgment in the temple court, Pashhur had him beaten and placed in stocks at the Upper Benjamin Gate — the first recorded physical persecution of the prophet. Jeremiah renamed him 'Magor-Missabib' (Terror on Every Side), prophesying that Pashhur would witness the destruction of everything he sought to protect and would die in Babylonian exile.
people.scriptureRole: Pashhur represents the institutional response to prophetic truth: when the message cannot be refuted, the messenger is assaulted. His story illustrates the recurring biblical pattern of religious authorities persecuting those who speak God's word.

people.name: Jehoiachin (Coniah)
people.role: King of Judah (597 BC, 3 months); exiled to Babylon
people.bio: Jehoiachin (also called Coniah and Jeconiah) succeeded his father Jehoiakim at age eighteen and reigned only three months before surrendering to Nebuchadnezzar in 597 BC. He was deported to Babylon along with 10,000 captives, including Ezekiel. Jeremiah pronounced that he would be 'recorded as childless' — not physically, since he had sons, but dynastically: none of his descendants would sit on David's throne. After 37 years in captivity, he was released from prison by Evil-Merodach and given a seat of honor at the Babylonian court (2 Kgs 25:27-30). Babylonian ration tablets discovered in the ruins of Babylon confirm his presence and provisions.
people.scriptureRole: Jehoiachin's dynastic curse (Jer 22:30) creates a theological crisis for messianic expectation, resolved in the NT through the distinction between Jesus's legal lineage through Joseph and biological descent through Mary. The reversal of the signet ring curse in Haggai 2:23, applied to Zerubbabel (Jehoiachin's grandson), signals that grace overcomes even the most severe judgment.

people.name: Hananiah son of Azzur
people.role: False prophet from Gibeon
people.bio: Hananiah son of Azzur was a prophet from Gibeon who publicly contradicted Jeremiah in the temple court, promising that within two years God would break Nebuchadnezzar's yoke and return the temple vessels and exiles. He dramatically broke the wooden yoke from Jeremiah's neck as a counter-sign-act. God responded through Jeremiah: the wooden yoke would be replaced with iron, and Hananiah would die within the year for preaching 'rebellion against the LORD.' He died two months later, vindicating Jeremiah's word.
people.scriptureRole: Hananiah represents the false prophet who tells people what they want to hear rather than what God has said. His confrontation with Jeremiah is the defining test case for discerning true from false prophecy in the Hebrew Bible.

people.name: Ebed-Melech the Cushite
people.role: Cushite court official who rescued Jeremiah
people.bio: Ebed-Melech ('servant of the king') was a Cushite (Ethiopian/Sudanese) official in the court of King Zedekiah. When Jeremiah was thrown into a mud-filled cistern to die, Ebed-Melech went directly to the king, denounced the officials' cruelty, obtained permission for a rescue, and personally pulled the prophet out using rags and ropes as padding. His courage stands in sharp contrast to Zedekiah's weakness and the officials' malice. God rewarded him with a personal oracle of salvation: 'I will save you because you trust in me' (Jer 39:18). A foreigner's faith shames a nation's faithlessness.
people.scriptureRole: Ebed-Melech represents the faithful outsider -- like Rahab, Ruth, and the Ethiopian eunuch -- who acts with more courage and compassion than God's own covenant people. His rescue of Jeremiah from the cistern and his reward of survival during the fall demonstrate that faith, not ethnicity, determines one's standing before God.

people.name: Gedaliah son of Ahikam
people.role: Governor of Judah after the fall of Jerusalem
people.bio: Gedaliah son of Ahikam son of Shaphan was appointed governor of Judah by Nebuchadnezzar after the fall of Jerusalem in 586 BC. He came from the influential Shaphan family that had supported both Josiah's reforms and Jeremiah's ministry. He established his administration at Mizpah and urged the remaining population to serve Babylon peacefully. He was assassinated by Ishmael son of Nethaniah, a member of the royal family, triggering the flight to Egypt that Jeremiah desperately opposed. The Fast of Gedaliah (Tsom Gedaliah) is still observed in Judaism on the third of Tishrei.
people.scriptureRole: Gedaliah represents the last hope for peaceful coexistence under Babylonian rule -- the path Jeremiah consistently advocated. His assassination and the subsequent flight to Egypt demonstrate the continued rejection of prophetic counsel even after the catastrophe Jeremiah predicted.

people.name: Buzi
people.role: Priest, father of Ezekiel
people.bio: A Zadokite priest and father of the prophet Ezekiel. Nothing is known of Buzi beyond this identification in Ezekiel 1:3, but his priestly lineage is theologically significant: Ezekiel inherited his priestly identity and vocabulary from his father's household.
people.scriptureRole: Father of Ezekiel, establishing the prophet's priestly credentials.

people.name: Jehoiachin
people.role: King of Judah (3 months), exiled to Babylon
people.bio: Son of Jehoiakim, Jehoiachin reigned only three months before surrendering to Nebuchadnezzar in 597 BC. He was deported to Babylon along with 10,000 captives including Ezekiel. Babylonian ration tablets confirm his presence in Babylon. He was eventually released from prison by Evil-Merodach (2 Kgs 25:27-30).
people.scriptureRole: The exiled king whose deportation date provides the chronological framework for Ezekiel's prophecies.

people.name: Pelatiah son of Benaiah
people.role: Leader in Jerusalem who died during Ezekiel's vision
people.bio: One of twenty-five leaders at the east gate of the temple who gave wicked counsel, advising the people to ignore prophetic warnings. He died suddenly while Ezekiel was prophesying against the group, confirming the lethal power of the prophetic word and terrifying Ezekiel into intercession.
people.scriptureRole: His sudden death during Ezekiel's vision functions as prophetic authentication — demonstrating that the word of God actively accomplishes what it declares.

people.name: Jaazaniah son of Shaphan
people.role: Elder who led secret idolatry in the temple
people.bio: One of seventy elders seen in Ezekiel's temple vision worshiping animal images in a secret chamber, each with a censer in hand. Possibly related to the Shaphan family that included supporters of Jeremiah (Ahikam, Gedaliah), making his apostasy all the more shocking — a prominent family divided between faithfulness and idolatry.
people.scriptureRole: Named specifically among the seventy elders engaging in secret animal worship in the temple, representing the corruption of Israel's leadership class.

people.name: Zedekiah
people.role: Last king of Judah before the fall of Jerusalem
people.bio: The last king of Judah, installed by Nebuchadnezzar as a puppet ruler after Jehoiachin's deportation. He reigned from 597-586 BC but rebelled against Babylon despite warnings from both Jeremiah and Ezekiel. He attempted to flee Jerusalem through a breach in the wall at night but was captured near Jericho, forced to watch his sons executed, then blinded and taken to Babylon in chains.
people.scriptureRole: Ezekiel prophesied his fate through sign-acts (12:1-16): the prince carrying exile baggage with covered face, captured in God's net and brought to Babylon without seeing it — fulfilled precisely when Nebuchadnezzar blinded him.

people.name: Jehoahaz
people.role: King of Judah (3 months), deported to Egypt
people.bio: Son of Josiah, Jehoahaz reigned only three months before Pharaoh Necho deported him to Egypt, where he died. He is likely the first lion cub in Ezekiel 19 who was captured and taken to Egypt with hooks.
people.scriptureRole: The first of the captured lion cubs in Ezekiel's lament (19:3-4), representing the beginning of the Davidic dynasty's final decline.

people.name: Oholah
people.role: Allegorical figure representing Samaria in Ezekiel 23
people.bio: Oholah ('her own tent') is Ezekiel's allegorical name for Samaria, the capital of the northern kingdom of Israel. In the extended marriage metaphor of Ezekiel 23, Oholah represents northern Israel's spiritual adultery through political alliances with Assyria and Egypt that led to the adoption of foreign gods. Her name suggests illegitimate worship — she set up 'her own tent' rather than worshipping at God's chosen sanctuary in Jerusalem. Oholah was 'handed over to her lovers' — the Assyrians destroyed Samaria in 722 BC.
people.scripture_role: Allegorical representation of Samaria's covenant unfaithfulness and destruction by Assyria.

people.name: Oholibah
people.role: Allegorical figure representing Jerusalem in Ezekiel 23
people.bio: Oholibah ('my tent is in her') is Ezekiel's allegorical name for Jerusalem, the capital of the southern kingdom of Judah. Her name carries a divine claim — God's tent (the Temple) dwells in her. Despite this greater privilege, Oholibah exceeded her sister Oholah's depravity, forming alliances with Assyria, Babylon, and Egypt that led to spiritual prostitution. Her sin was worse because she witnessed Samaria's punishment and still refused to repent. She will drink the same cup of judgment her sister drank.
people.scripture_role: Allegorical representation of Jerusalem's covenant unfaithfulness, worse than Samaria's because of greater privilege.

people.name: Hiram of Tyre
people.role: King of Tyre; Solomon's ally in building the Temple
people.bio: Hiram (also Huram) was king of the Phoenician city-state of Tyre during the reigns of David and Solomon. He formed a commercial and diplomatic alliance with Israel, supplying cedar wood, skilled craftsmen, and gold for the construction of Solomon's Temple and palace in exchange for wheat and olive oil. This alliance represented the height of Israelite-Phoenician cooperation and makes Tyre's later gloating over Jerusalem's fall (Ezek 26:2) all the more bitter — the city that helped build the Temple celebrated its destruction.
people.scripture_role: Allied king who supplied materials and craftsmen for Solomon's Temple construction.

people.name: Pharaoh Hophra (Apries)
people.role: Pharaoh of Egypt during Jerusalem's fall
people.bio: Pharaoh Hophra (Egyptian name Wahibre, Greek Apries) was the fourth king of Egypt's 26th Dynasty. He attempted to relieve the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem in 588 BC but quickly withdrew when Nebuchadnezzar turned to engage his forces. This military failure is the 'broken arm' of Ezekiel 30:21. He was eventually overthrown by his general Amasis in 570 BC. Ezekiel's Egypt oracles (chs. 29-32) are largely directed against him.
people.scriptureRole: The pharaoh addressed in Ezekiel's Egypt oracles, portrayed as a sea monster (tannin) lurking in the Nile who claims divine creative power. His failed attempt to relieve Jerusalem and subsequent military decline fulfilled Ezekiel's prophecies of Egypt's judgment.

people.name: Gog
people.role: Archetypal enemy of God's restored people; prince of Magog
people.bio: Gog of the land of Magog is identified as the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal (Ezek 38:2). His identity has been debated for millennia: proposals include Gyges of Lydia (7th c. BC), a symbolic name from Sumerian gug (darkness), or a purely eschatological figure without historical referent. In Ezekiel 38-39, Gog leads a vast coalition from the far north against restored Israel, only to be annihilated by direct divine intervention through earthquake, plague, hailstones, and fire. His defeat clears the way for the temple vision (chs. 40-48). Revelation 20:7-10 borrows Gog and Magog as shorthand for the final post-millennial rebellion of nations against God's people.
people.scriptureRole: Gog functions as the archetypal final enemy whose defeat demonstrates God's absolute sovereignty over all opposition. His coalition assembles peoples from the Table of Nations (Gen 10), representing the extremities of the known world converging against God's people. His destruction by cosmic weaponry (earthquake, fire, sulfur) parallels the destruction of Sodom and the plagues of Egypt, establishing a pattern that Revelation 19-20 adopts wholesale.

people.name: Zadok
people.role: High Priest under David and Solomon
people.bio: Zadok was a priest of the line of Eleazar, son of Aaron, who served under David alongside Abiathar. He remained loyal during Absalom's rebellion (2 Sam 15:24-29) and anointed Solomon as king (1 Kgs 1:39). When Abiathar supported Adonijah's rival claim, Solomon removed Abiathar and elevated Zadok as sole high priest (1 Kgs 2:26-27, 35), fulfilling the prophecy against the house of Eli (1 Sam 2:35). His descendants controlled the high priesthood through the pre-exilic, exilic, and Second Temple periods. In Ezekiel's temple vision, only the 'sons of Zadok' retain full altar access because they remained faithful when other Levites went astray (Ezek 44:15-16). The Zadokite priesthood became the standard of legitimate priestly authority in post-exilic Judaism.
people.scriptureRole: High Priest; ancestor of the exclusive altar-serving priestly line in Ezekiel's eschatological temple

people.name: Jonah
people.role: Prophet to Nineveh; reluctant messenger of divine mercy
people.bio: Jonah son of Amittai was a prophet from Gath-hepher in the territory of Zebulun in the northern kingdom of Israel. He prophesied during the reign of Jeroboam II (2 Kgs 14:25), predicting the restoration of Israel's borders — a nationalistic oracle that makes his commission to preach mercy to Nineveh, Israel's greatest enemy, all the more shocking. The book bearing his name is unique among the Twelve: it is narrative rather than oracle, and its protagonist is an anti-hero who flees his calling, is swallowed by a great fish, reluctantly preaches, and then rages at God's compassion. Jesus cited Jonah's three days in the fish as a sign of his own death and resurrection (Matt 12:39-41), and Jonah's story became one of the most depicted scenes in early Christian art.
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Jeroboam II
people.role: King of Israel; longest and most prosperous reign of the northern kingdom
people.bio: Jeroboam II son of Jehoash reigned over the northern kingdom of Israel for forty-one years, the longest reign of any Israelite king. He restored Israel's borders from Lebo-hamath to the Dead Sea, fulfilling the word of Jonah son of Amittai (2 Kgs 14:25). His reign was a period of unprecedented prosperity and territorial expansion, but the prophets Amos and Hosea condemned the social injustice and religious corruption that flourished under his rule. The wealth of the elite came at the expense of the poor, and the cult sites at Bethel and Dan perpetuated the idolatry of Jeroboam I. His death marked the beginning of the northern kingdom's rapid decline toward the Assyrian conquest of 722 BC.
people.scriptureRole: King

people.name: Amos
people.role: Shepherd-prophet; voice of social justice from Tekoa
people.bio: Amos was a shepherd and tender of sycamore-fig trees from Tekoa, a village about ten miles south of Jerusalem in the Judean wilderness. Though a southerner, he was called by God to prophesy against the northern kingdom of Israel during the prosperous reign of Jeroboam II (c. 760-750 BC). He was the earliest of the writing prophets, and his message was explosive: YHWH's covenant demands social justice, and Israel's religious observance without ethical conduct is an abomination. His confrontation with Amaziah, the priest of Bethel (7:10-17), dramatizes the clash between prophetic authority and institutional religion. His oracles against the nations (chs. 1-2) demonstrate that YHWH's moral authority extends beyond Israel, and his five visions (chs. 7-9) trace the progression from mercy to inevitable judgment.
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Amaziah (Priest of Bethel)
people.role: Priest of the royal sanctuary at Bethel; opponent of Amos
people.bio: Amaziah was the priest of the royal sanctuary at Bethel, the primary cultic center of the northern kingdom of Israel. When Amos delivered oracles of judgment against Israel and its king Jeroboam II, Amaziah reported the prophet to the king and ordered Amos to leave Bethel and return to Judah. His words to Amos ('Get out, you seer! Go back to the land of Judah. Earn your bread there and do your prophesying there. Don\'t prophesy anymore at Bethel, because this is the king\'s sanctuary and the temple of the kingdom') reveal the fusion of religious and political power that Amos condemned. Amos responded with a devastating oracle against Amaziah's own family (7:17).
people.scriptureRole: Priest

people.name: Hosea
people.role: Prophet to the northern kingdom; enacted God's covenant love through his marriage to the unfaithful Gomer
people.bio: Hosea son of Beeri is the only writing prophet known to have been a native of the northern kingdom of Israel. His prophetic ministry spanned roughly four decades during Israel's final years, from the prosperous reign of Jeroboam II through the political chaos of Israel's last kings until the fall of Samaria to Assyria in 722 BC. God commanded Hosea to marry Gomer, a woman of harlotry, as a living parable of YHWH's relationship with faithless Israel. Hosea's three children received symbolic names: Jezreel (God sows/scatters), Lo-Ruhamah (not pitied), and Lo-Ammi (not my people). The book moves from biographical narrative (chs. 1-3) to oracular poetry (chs. 4-14), developing a theology of divine love (hesed) that persists despite betrayal. Hosea's marriage metaphor profoundly influenced later prophets, especially Jeremiah and Ezekiel, and his covenant theology anticipates the new covenant language of the NT.
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Gomer
people.role: Wife of Hosea; symbol of unfaithful Israel in the prophetic marriage allegory
people.bio: Gomer daughter of Diblaim was the wife of the prophet Hosea. God commanded Hosea to take a 'wife of harlotry' (eshet zenunim) as a prophetic sign-act embodying Israel's spiritual adultery against YHWH. The precise nature of Gomer's unfaithfulness is debated: some scholars view her as a cult prostitute, others as an ordinary woman who later proved unfaithful, and still others interpret the description proleptically — she became adulterous after the marriage. Gomer bore three children whose names carried oracular significance. In Hosea 3, the prophet is commanded to love her again despite her adultery, purchasing her back from slavery or a lover for fifteen shekels of silver and measures of barley. This act of redemption became a powerful theological metaphor for divine grace reclaiming an unfaithful people.
people.scriptureRole: Wife of Prophet

people.name: Beeri
people.role: Father of the prophet Hosea
people.bio: Beeri is known only as the father of the prophet Hosea (Hos 1:1). The name means 'my well' or 'spring' in Hebrew. Nothing else is recorded about him. He should not be confused with Beeri the Hittite, father of Judith, one of Esau's wives (Gen 26:34).
people.scriptureRole: Father of Prophet

people.name: Jezreel (son of Hosea)
people.role: First child of Hosea and Gomer; symbolic name foreshadowing judgment on the house of Jehu
people.bio: Jezreel was the firstborn son of Hosea and Gomer. His name, meaning 'God sows' or 'God scatters,' carried a double-edged significance. It recalled the Jezreel Valley where Jehu had conducted his bloody purge of the house of Ahab (2 Kgs 9-10), and it signaled that God would soon punish the house of Jehu for that bloodshed and break Israel's military power in the same valley. Yet in Hosea 2:22-23 the name is reversed: God will 'sow' Israel in the land as an act of restoration, turning the symbol of judgment into a symbol of renewed blessing.
people.scriptureRole: Child of Prophet

people.name: Lo-Ruhamah
people.role: Second child of Hosea and Gomer; symbolic name meaning 'not loved' or 'not pitied'
people.bio: Lo-Ruhamah was the second child born to Gomer during her marriage to Hosea. The name means 'not loved' or 'not pitied' (from the root raham, 'to have compassion'). God declared that he would no longer show compassion to the house of Israel, though he would still spare Judah. The text notably omits 'she bore him a daughter' (contrast 1:3), leading some scholars to suggest Gomer's infidelity had already begun and Lo-Ruhamah may not have been Hosea's biological child. In Hosea 2:1 and 2:23, the name is reversed: God will have compassion on 'Not-Pitied' and call her 'My-Loved-One' (Ruhamah). Paul cites this reversal in Romans 9:25-26.
people.scriptureRole: Child of Prophet

people.name: Lo-Ammi
people.role: Third child of Hosea and Gomer; symbolic name meaning 'not my people'
people.bio: Lo-Ammi was the third child born to Gomer. The name means 'not my people' and constitutes the most devastating of the three symbolic names. It reverses the covenant formula of Exodus 6:7 ('I will take you as my own people, and I will be your God'), effectively announcing covenant dissolution. God declares, 'you are not my people, and I am not your God' (or more literally, 'I am not your I AM' — a play on the divine name from Ex 3:14). Like the other names, Lo-Ammi is reversed in the restoration oracle of Hosea 2:23: 'I will say to those called Not-My-People, You are my people.' Peter applies this reversal to the inclusion of Gentiles in the new covenant community (1 Pet 2:10).
people.scriptureRole: Child of Prophet

people.name: Micah
people.role: Prophet from Moresheth; champion of social justice and messianic hope
people.bio: Micah of Moresheth was a prophet from the Judean Shephelah (lowlands) who ministered during the reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah. He was a contemporary of Isaiah in Jerusalem and Hosea in the north. Unlike the aristocratic Isaiah, Micah spoke from the perspective of rural Judah, experiencing firsthand the economic exploitation condemned in his oracles. His prophecy of Jerusalem's destruction (3:12) was remembered over a century later and cited by name in Jeremiah 26:18 — the only instance of one writing prophet quoting another by name. Micah is best known for the ethical summary of 6:8 ('act justly, love mercy, walk humbly with your God') and for the Bethlehem oracle of 5:2, which Matthew applies to the birth of Jesus (Matt 2:6). His book alternates between judgment oracles against corrupt leadership and stunning visions of eschatological restoration, concluding with a hymn to divine forgiveness (7:18-20) that plays on his own name (mi-kha-el, 'who is like God?').
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Habakkuk
people.role: Prophet who questioned God's justice and received the revelation that 'the righteous will live by faith'
people.bio: Habakkuk is unique among the prophets in that his book records not oracles addressed to the people but a dialogue with God. Rather than delivering divine messages to Israel, Habakkuk interrogates YHWH about the problem of evil: why does God tolerate injustice in Judah, and why would he use an even more wicked nation (Babylon) as his instrument of judgment? The divine answer — 'the righteous person will live by his faithfulness' (2:4) — became one of the most influential verses in the Bible, cited by Paul (Rom 1:17; Gal 3:11) and the author of Hebrews (10:38) as the foundation of justification by faith. The book's third chapter is a theophanic psalm of extraordinary power, describing God's march through the cosmos as divine warrior, and concluding with one of the most celebrated declarations of faith in Scripture: 'Though the fig tree does not bud... yet I will rejoice in the LORD' (3:17-18).
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Nahum
people.role: Prophet who proclaimed the destruction of Nineveh as divine vengeance for Assyrian cruelty
people.bio: Nahum the Elkoshite is identified only by his superscription (1:1). The location of Elkosh remains debated: proposals include al-Qosh near Mosul in modern Iraq, Capernaum ('village of Nahum') in Galilee, and Beit Jibrin in southern Judah. His name, from the Hebrew root nkh, means 'comfort' or 'consolation' — an ironic designation for a prophet whose entire book announces Nineveh's annihilation. Nahum prophesied between the fall of Thebes to Ashurbanipal in 663 BC (referenced in 3:8-10 as a past event) and the fall of Nineveh to the Babylonian-Median coalition in 612 BC. His poetry ranks among the most vivid and powerful in the prophetic corpus, employing rapid shifts in imagery, staccato rhythm, and theophanic language drawn from the Divine Warrior tradition. The book functions as a companion piece to Jonah: where Jonah announced judgment that was averted by repentance, Nahum announces judgment that is irrevocable.
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Zephaniah
people.role: Prophet of royal lineage who proclaimed the coming Day of the LORD and the hope of a purified remnant
people.bio: Zephaniah son of Cushi, son of Gedaliah, son of Amariah, son of Hezekiah (1:1) possesses the longest genealogy of any writing prophet — four generations tracing his ancestry to King Hezekiah of Judah. This royal lineage gave him unique access to the court of Josiah, in whose reign he prophesied. His name means 'the LORD hides' or 'the LORD treasures' (from tsaphan + Yah), which resonates with the book's call to 'seek shelter' before the day of the LORD's wrath (2:3). Zephaniah's message combines sweeping cosmic judgment — the undoing of creation itself (1:2-3) — with fierce denunciation of Jerusalem's religious syncretism (Baal worship, astral cults, Molek rites) and social injustice. Yet the book pivots to a stunning vision of restoration: God will purify the nations, preserve a humble remnant, and dwell among his people as a rejoicing warrior-king (3:14-20). The 'Day of the LORD' theology in Zephaniah profoundly influenced later apocalyptic literature and the medieval hymn Dies Irae.
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Haggai
people.role: Post-exilic prophet who urged the returned exiles to rebuild the Jerusalem temple
people.bio: Haggai is one of the two post-exilic prophets (with Zechariah) whose ministry is independently attested in the historical books (Ezra 5:1; 6:14). His name means 'festive' or 'my feast' (from hag), possibly indicating birth during a pilgrimage festival. His four precisely dated oracles span only three months in the second year of the Persian king Darius I (August-December 520 BC), making his the most precisely dated prophetic ministry in the Hebrew Bible. Haggai's message was blunt and practical: the returned exiles had rebuilt their own paneled houses while leaving the LORD's temple in ruins (1:4, 9), and their economic hardships — drought, crop failure, inflation — were divine judgment for this misplaced priority (1:5-11). His rhetoric of 'consider your ways' (1:5, 7; 2:15, 18) challenged the community to action, and the people responded with obedience within twenty-three days (1:12-15). The second oracle promises that the glory of the rebuilt temple will surpass Solomon's (2:6-9), and the final oracle designates Zerubbabel as God's chosen 'signet ring' — reversing the rejection of Jehoiachin's ring in Jeremiah 22:24.
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Joshua son of Jozadak
people.role: First high priest of the rebuilt Jerusalem temple after the Babylonian exile
people.bio: Joshua (also called Jeshua) son of Jozadak (or Jehozadak) served as the first high priest of the post-exilic community. His father Jozadak had been deported to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar (1 Chr 6:15). Joshua returned with Zerubbabel in the first wave of exiles under Cyrus's decree (Ezra 2:2; Neh 7:7) and immediately set about restoring the altar and reinstituting sacrificial worship (Ezra 3:2-6). Together with the governor Zerubbabel, he led the effort to rebuild the temple, overcoming Samaritan opposition and prophetic encouragement from Haggai and Zechariah (Ezra 5:1-2). In Zechariah's visions, Joshua is dramatically portrayed standing before the angel of the LORD, with Satan accusing him; he is cleansed, reclothed in priestly garments, and given authority over the temple courts (Zech 3:1-7). The symbolic crowning of Joshua (Zech 6:11) merges priestly and royal imagery, prefiguring the messianic 'Branch' who will unite both offices.
people.scriptureRole: High Priest

people.name: Darius I
people.role: Persian king who authorized the completion of the Second Temple in Jerusalem
people.bio: Darius I (the Great), son of Hystaspes, seized the Persian throne in 522 BC after the death of Cambyses II and the brief usurpation of Gaumata. He reorganized the empire into twenty satrapies, built the magnificent capital at Persepolis, and expanded Persian territory to its greatest extent. For biblical history, his most significant act was reaffirming the decree of Cyrus permitting the Jews to rebuild the Jerusalem temple. When local officials challenged the Jews' building activity, Darius ordered a search of the royal archives at Ecbatana, where Cyrus's original decree was found (Ezra 6:1-5). Darius not only confirmed the decree but added royal financing from the provincial treasury and threatened severe penalties against anyone who interfered (Ezra 6:6-12). The temple was completed in the sixth year of Darius, 515 BC (Ezra 6:15). The prophetic ministries of both Haggai and Zechariah are dated to his second year (520 BC). Darius is also known for his failed invasion of Greece, culminating in the Battle of Marathon (490 BC).
people.scriptureRole: King

people.name: Zechariah (prophet)
people.role: Post-exilic prophet and priest who encouraged the returned exiles through apocalyptic visions and messianic oracles
people.bio: Zechariah son of Berekiah, son of Iddo, was a prophet and priest who began prophesying in the eighth month of the second year of Darius I (October/November 520 BC), two months after Haggai's first oracle. His name means 'Yahweh remembers' (from zakar). He belonged to the priestly clan of Iddo (Neh 12:4, 16), and Ezra identifies him alongside Haggai as the prophetic catalyst for the temple rebuilding (Ezra 5:1; 6:14). His book is the longest of the Minor Prophets and divides into two major sections: chapters 1-8 contain eight night visions and associated oracles dated between 520 and 518 BC, while chapters 9-14 comprise undated oracles of eschatological judgment and messianic restoration. The night visions employ rich symbolism — horsemen, lampstands, flying scrolls, four chariots — mediated by an interpreting angel (malʾakh), a feature shared with later apocalyptic literature. Zechariah's messianic theology is among the most developed in the Hebrew Bible, introducing the 'Branch' (tsemach) figure (3:8; 6:12), the pierced one whom the nations will mourn (12:10), and the shepherd struck down by God's sword (13:7). The New Testament draws extensively on Zechariah, particularly in the Passion narratives (Matt 21:5; 26:31; 27:9; John 19:37; Rev 1:7).
people.scriptureRole: Prophet

people.name: Malachi
people.role: Last of the Old Testament prophets; confronted priestly corruption, intermarriage, and spiritual apathy in post-exilic Judah
people.bio: Malachi ('my messenger') is either the personal name or the prophetic title of the author of the last book of the twelve Minor Prophets. His ministry dates to the mid-fifth century BC, after the Second Temple was completed (515 BC) but during a period of spiritual decline that Nehemiah later sought to reform (Neh 13). The community had grown disillusioned with covenant life: the priests offered blemished sacrifices (1:6-14), the men divorced their Jewish wives to marry pagan women (2:10-16), tithes went unpaid (3:8-10), and the people questioned whether serving God was worthwhile (3:14-15). Malachi's distinctive literary technique is the disputation oracle — a dialogical form in which God makes a statement, the people challenge it with 'How?' or 'Why?', and God responds with evidence and judgment. The book closes the Old Testament prophetic canon with the promise of Elijah's return before 'the great and dreadful day of the LORD' (4:5-6), a prophecy the New Testament identifies with John the Baptist (Matt 11:14; 17:10-13; Luke 1:17).
people.scriptureRole: Prophet
